apps.name: Chuckl
apps.tagline: Your personal reaction library — browse, stash, deploy.
apps.overview: A utility app that serves as a personal hub for reaction GIFs and images. Users browse a curated library organized by category/mood, stash their favorites for instant access, and copy them to clipboard to use in any chat or comment. No social feed — pure utility.
apps.painPoint: People want to use the perfect reaction GIF or image when commenting on social media or in chats, but there is no clean place to organize and access them. Camera rolls get cluttered, GIPHY is low-quality and inconsistent, and finding the right reaction in the moment is friction-heavy.
strengths: Clear, specific pain point that people actually feel
No direct competitor solving this exact utility use case
Simple core loop: browse → stash → copy → share
Freemium model with natural conversion trigger (stash limit)
Content sourced from community submissions — scales without ongoing work
React Native allows cross-platform expansion later
challenges: Content cold start — need a quality seed library before launch
Content moderation — approving user submissions takes ongoing effort
Discovery — how do users find the app? Marketing matters
GIPHY/Tenor are free alternatives for GIFs (Chuckl's value is curation + stash UX, not just GIF access)
Apple App Store review process for user-generated content apps
apps.monetization: Freemium: free tier limited to 20-30 stashed reactions. Premium tier: $2.99/month for unlimited stash. Conversion trigger hits at the right moment — when users are most engaged and have hit their limit.
apps.timeline: Phase 1: Backend/Supabase (1-2 weeks) → Phase 2: React Native build (2-3 weeks) → Phase 3: Content + Polish + TestFlight (2 weeks) → App Store submission. Total: 6-8 weeks.
apps.techStack: React Native (iOS first), Supabase (auth + database + storage), Stripe (payments), Expo (build/deploy)
apps.gamePlan:
## Current Status
Prototype live at chuckl.vercel.app — concept validated. Moving to real build.

## Decisions Made

### Tech Stack
- **React Native** (not Swift) — same JS/React codebase, compiles to native iOS. Faster build, Patch knows it, can expand to Android later.
- **Supabase** — backend for auth, database, and image/GIF storage. Free tier sufficient to start.
- **No prototype polish** — React Native is a full rebuild. UI spec will be written first, then built right.

### Content Moderation
- **Open uploads** — all content goes live immediately, no approval bottleneck
- **Trusted Curators (Premium Users)** — a small group (Zach + designated friends) can mark content as "Featured" — fast-tracked to top spots
- **Community flagging** — users flag bad content, reviewed and removed
- **Two-tier content:** Featured (curator-approved, top of feed/library) vs Standard (everything else)

### Monetization
- Free tier: 30 stash limit
- Premium: $2.99/month for unlimited stash
- Freemium conversion trigger at stash limit

### App Store
- Apple Developer account: Zach already has one ✅
- Content library: Zach + friends curate 100-200 seed reactions pre-launch ✅

---

## Build Phases

### Phase 1 — Backend Setup (1-2 weeks)
- [ ] Zach creates Supabase account (supabase.com)
- [ ] Wally sets up database schema (reactions, users, stash, flags tables)
- [ ] Set up Supabase Storage buckets (reactions, avatars)
- [ ] Configure Supabase Auth (email + Apple Sign-In)
- [ ] Get Supabase API keys

### Phase 2 — React Native Build (2-3 weeks)
- [ ] Patch scaffolds React Native + Expo project
- [ ] Feed screen (newest reactions, open uploads, + button)
- [ ] Library screen (browse by category, sort by stash count/trending/new)
- [ ] Profile screen (3-col stash gallery, search, category filter, swipeable viewer)
- [ ] Auth screens (sign up, log in, Apple Sign-In)
- [ ] Stash mechanic (real DB, synced to account)
- [ ] Freemium stash limit + upgrade prompt
- [ ] Submit/upload flow
- [ ] Curator/featured content system

### Phase 3 — Content + Polish + Launch (2 weeks)
- [ ] Seed library: Zach + friends curate 100-200 top reactions across all categories
- [ ] TestFlight beta with small group
- [ ] App Store submission
- [ ] Launch

---

## Next Immediate Actions
1. Wally writes UI spec document (screens, flows, exact behavior)
2. Zach sets up Supabase account
3. Wally configures Supabase schema with Zach's credentials
4. Patch begins React Native scaffold
apps.notes:
App icon: chuckl-icon.png (transparent background, red laughing face)
Prototype URL: https://chuckl.vercel.app
GitHub: https://github.com/zhunton/chuckl
Apple Developer: Zach has account

Key product decisions:
- Open uploads, curator-elevated "Featured" system
- React Native not Swift
- Supabase backend
- $2.99/month premium tier, 30 stash free limit

apps.name: VantaScout
apps.tagline: A map of every HVAC asset you can sell service on.
apps.overview: A web-based tool that uses satellite/aerial imagery and AI computer vision to detect commercial HVAC equipment (cooling towers, RTUs, chillers) on building rooftops. Cross-references with property data to generate qualified leads for HVAC service companies — identifying buildings with aging or detectable equipment before competitors do.
apps.painPoint: HVAC contractors don't know which buildings have equipment they could be maintaining. Generic lead tools tell you a building is 100,000 sqft — but contractors think in units: 'How many RTUs do I have to maintain? What's the service footprint?' No tool answers that question. VantaScout does.
strengths: No direct competitor identified — this exact market gap is real and unoccupied
Adjacent industries (solar, roofing) have proven satellite lead-gen works
AI accuracy for rooftop equipment detection proven in research (87-91% mAP)
Equipment COUNT is the killer feature — contractors price per unit, plan labor per unit, sell contracts per unit
Service agreement prospecting = recurring revenue model for contractors — far more valuable than one-off leads
Hunton Group is the ideal first customer — internal validation with no sales friction
Enables internal gap analysis: compare detected equipment vs. existing customer base to find missed accounts
Scoring model potential: equipment count × type × building size → '$25K/year service opportunity'
Three distinct revenue use cases means three different buyer personas to sell to
challenges: Labeled training data is limited — academic datasets are small, need custom annotation at scale
High-resolution aerial imagery is expensive (Nearmap, EagleView enterprise pricing not disclosed)
Satellite resolution varies — rooftop equipment requires high-res imagery (15cm GSD or better)
Building in Houston first limits scale — aerial imagery coverage varies by city
Equipment age cannot be determined from imagery alone — additional data needed for true lead scoring
Integration with property ownership data (who to call) requires additional data APIs
Regulatory/privacy considerations for commercial aerial imagery use
apps.monetization:
Two viable models:
1. **SaaS subscription for HVAC contractors** — $299-999/month for a lead territory (e.g., Houston commercial). Contractor gets a dashboard of flagged buildings with addresses and equipment type.
2. **Internal tool first** — Build for Hunton Group exclusively, prove ROI, then productize and sell to other HVAC companies. Internal use removes the sales problem entirely at launch.
apps.timeline:
Phase 1 (4-6 weeks): Proof of concept using existing academic models on Houston aerial imagery
Phase 2 (8-12 weeks): MVP with custom training data and Nearmap API integration
Phase 3: Build lead enrichment layer (property owner data, contact info)
Phase 4: Package as SaaS product for external contractors
apps.techStack: Python (PyTorch/Detectron2 for AI model), Nearmap or EagleView API (aerial imagery), Google Maps API (building data/display), PostgreSQL + PostGIS (geospatial data), React (frontend dashboard), Supabase (backend), Stripe (billing for SaaS version)
apps.gamePlan:
## Three Revenue Use Cases (Priority Order)

### 1. Service Agreement Prospecting (MOST IMPORTANT)
A building with 8 RTUs and 2 cooling towers = a high-value recurring revenue target. Contractors want to know the full serviceable footprint before making a call. VantaScout gives them that instantly.

Why this matters most:
- Service agreements are recurring revenue (predictable, high-margin)
- Pricing and labor planning are per-unit — contractors NEED unit counts
- Contract value scales directly with equipment footprint
- This is the question no other lead tool answers

### 2. Replacement / Capital Projects
- Identify visually degraded or aging equipment
- Flag older buildings with older systems
- Large systems = bigger ticket jobs
- High margin but lower frequency than service agreements

### 3. New Construction / Retrofit Awareness (future)
- Detect new installs
- Track recently upgraded buildings
- Monitor competitor activity

---

## Core Positioning

**DO NOT position as:** "AI that detects old equipment"

**POSITION AS:** "A map of every HVAC asset you can sell service on"

The aging/replacement signal is a secondary layer. The primary value is: here are all the buildings with HVAC equipment, here's how much equipment they have, here's what type. Everything else flows from that.

---

## Internal Use Case (Hunton Group)

Testing this internally first unlocks a powerful secondary benefit:
- Compare VantaScout detections vs. existing customer database
- Find: buildings you're not serving, accounts with under-serviced equipment
- Prioritize sales reps based on true opportunity size
- Build the scoring model on real data before productizing

---

## Lead Scoring Model (future)
Score each detected building:
- Equipment count → revenue potential
- Equipment type → complexity/value tier
- Building size → budget likelihood
- Location → service route efficiency
- Output: "This building is a $25K/year service opportunity"

---

## Recommended Path
1. Build proof of concept on Houston commercial buildings
2. Test internally at Hunton Group — compare detections to existing accounts
3. Prove ROI (2-3 service agreements won from VantaScout leads)
4. Build the scoring model
5. Productize and sell to other HVAC contractors nationally

---

## Research (Atlas)
- Academic prototypes: 87-91% mAP for RTU detection from aerial imagery
- Adjacent: GetSolar, Nearmap, FatCamel use same model for solar/roofing leads
- No commercial HVAC-specific rooftop detection tool exists
- Imagery: Nearmap (multiple annual captures, US urban), EagleView, Google Maps
- Property data: ATTOM, CoreLogic, Melissa Data for owner contact info
apps.notes:
Key open questions before building:
1. Nearmap enterprise API cost for Houston commercial coverage?
2. Labeled dataset availability for Houston rooftop HVAC training?
3. Best property data API for building owner contact info (ATTOM, CoreLogic)?

First validation step (1-day, no cost): Pull Houston commercial buildings via Google Maps, manually identify cooling towers, test Detectron2 pre-trained model on them.

Internal name: VantaScout

apps.name: Octaply
apps.tagline: AI-powered career platform — resume optimization, keyword scoring, job tracking, cover letters.
apps.overview: Octaply was Zach's previous company — an AI-powered job search platform that automatically scanned job boards, matched listings to user profiles, tailored resumes to each application, and submitted applications automatically. Shut down due to developer costs and the technical volatility of bypassing job board anti-scraping protections. Zach is evaluating whether to relaunch with an AI agent team.
apps.painPoint: Job seekers spend 10+ hours per week manually finding and applying to jobs. The application process is repetitive, soul-crushing, and largely unchanged despite the existence of AI tools that could automate it. Most people apply to 10-20 jobs manually when they need to apply to 100+ to get interviews at meaningful rates.
strengths: Zach has domain expertise and prior product experience — not starting from zero
GitHub repos with existing code still exist — foundation to build from
AI coding tools (Claude Code, Patch) dramatically reduce developer cost vs. original build
Demand is proven — competitors are generating real revenue
Resume tailoring + keyword optimization is a genuinely useful AI application
Market is growing — AI recruitment market $706M in 2025, projected $1.12B by 2032
challenges: BIGGEST RISK: Job boards (LinkedIn, Indeed, ZipRecruiter) aggressively block automated scrapers — rate limiting, CAPTCHA, device fingerprinting, ML bot detection, legal threats (CFAA)
AI agents still struggle to bypass anti-scraping at scale — no reported high success rates in 2026
Constant maintenance required as job boards update defenses — this was what killed Octaply originally
Legal risk: ToS violations, CFAA exposure if bypassing CAPTCHers or login walls, GDPR implications
Churn challenge: users leave the moment they get a job — inherently high churn product
Competitive market: AIApply, Sonara, LazyApply, Teal, Simplify all competing
Application spam problem: automated mass applying degrades response rates and reputation
apps.monetization: Subscription + credits model (industry standard). AIApply charges $29/month base + $10/10 application credits. Alternative: $49-99/month all-inclusive. Resume tailoring + job coaching add-on tiers. One-time packages (Scale.jobs charges $199-$1,099). Key question: can unit economics work given AI agent cost per application?
apps.timeline: Strategic decision needed before any build timeline. Recommend: 1) Technical feasibility test (2 weeks) before committing, 2) If viable: MVP rebuild 6-8 weeks with Patch.
apps.techStack: React/Next.js frontend, Supabase backend, Claude/GPT for resume tailoring, Playwright/Puppeteer for browser automation, residential proxy service, CAPTCHA solving service (2captcha/Anti-Captcha)
apps.gamePlan:
## Wally's Strategic Assessment

### The Core Question: Is the Technical Problem Solvable?

This is the make-or-break question and it needs an honest answer before anything else.

**The bad news:** Job board anti-scraping has gotten significantly better. LinkedIn, Indeed, and ZipRecruiter now use:
- ML-based bot detection (behavioral analysis, not just CAPTCHA)
- Device fingerprinting
- Rate limiting that adapts to usage patterns
- Login walls that require real accounts
- Aggressive legal action against scrapers (hiQ Labs precedent is limited)

Modern AI agents (GPT-4, Claude) cannot reliably bypass these at scale in 2026. Attempts often fail due to dynamic defense updates, requiring constant maintenance — exactly what burned Octaply before.

**The somewhat better news:** 
1. Residential proxies + headless browsers (Playwright) with human-like behavior patterns have higher success rates than pure algorithms
2. Some job boards have official APIs or partnership programs that could be negotiated
3. The market is tolerating imperfect automation — competitors exist and charge real money, suggesting users accept partial success

### Competitor Reality Check

**AIApply:** $29/month + $10/10 application credits. Auto-apply described as "slow and inaccurate" by users. Weak location targeting. No free trial. Strong user complaints about pricing transparency. Pre-seed funded March 2024 — still burning investor money, likely not profitable.

**Teal:** 3.2 million users. Freemium. Focuses on job tracking and resume optimization more than auto-apply. Not a pure automation play.

**LazyApply, Sonara:** Exist but insufficient public data on performance.

**Key competitive insight:** Nobody in this space is crushing it technically. The auto-apply accuracy is mediocre across the board. The companies that are surviving are leaning on resume tools, job coaching, and interview prep — not pure application automation.

### Wally's Honest Take: Three Paths

**Path A — Full Relaunch (High Risk, High Reward)**
Rebuild Octaply as originally conceived. Full auto-apply, job scraping, resume tailoring. Requires solving the scraping problem. Risk: same technical volatility that killed it before. Might work if you can negotiate official API access with job boards.

**Path B — Pivot: Resume + Career Intelligence (Lower Risk, Lower Ceiling)**
Skip the auto-apply entirely. Focus on what AI does well without legal/technical risk:
- Resume builder with AI optimization
- ATS keyword scoring
- Job market intelligence (salary data, in-demand skills)
- Interview prep
- Application tracker dashboard

This is what Teal does. 3.2M users. Proven model. Smaller TAM but buildable.

**Path C — Hybrid (Recommended)**
Build the resume/career platform first (Path B). It's buildable now with Patch in 4-6 weeks. Add auto-apply as a premium feature where technically feasible — start with job boards that allow it (some niche boards have APIs). Don't promise what you can't deliver, but offer it where you can.

This gets you to market faster, generates revenue, and lets you solve the scraping problem incrementally rather than betting everything on it at launch.

### The AI Agent Cost Question

Resume tailoring via Claude API: ~$0.01-0.03 per resume (very cheap)
Job matching/screening: ~$0.01-0.02 per listing analyzed
Application submission (agent): $0.05-0.20 per successful application attempt (includes proxy costs, retries, failure rate)
CAPTCHA solving services: $0.001-0.005 per solve

At $10 for 10 applications (AIApply's pricing), you'd need to keep total cost per application under $0.50-0.80 to have margin. Resume tailoring + AI matching is easy. Browser automation at scale with retries and proxy costs is where it gets tight — not impossible but thin.

### Recommendation

Do NOT rebuild Octaply exactly as it was. The scraping problem is harder than before.

DO consider building the career/resume intelligence layer first. Validate that people will pay. Then layer in auto-apply where technically feasible.

**First step before any decision:** Spend 2 weeks testing whether browser automation can reliably submit applications to Indeed and LinkedIn in 2026 with Playwright + residential proxies. If the success rate is >70%, the economics work. If it's <50%, Path B is the safer bet.

---

## Competitor Summary Table

| Platform | Monthly Price | Auto-Apply | Resume Tools | Key Weakness |
|----------|--------------|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| AIApply | $29 + $10/10 apps | Yes (slow/inaccurate) | Yes | Pricing opacity, accuracy |
| Teal | Free / Premium | No | Yes (strong) | No auto-apply |
| LazyApply | ~$49-99/month | Yes | Limited | Unknown accuracy |
| Sonara | Unknown | Yes | Unknown | Limited data |
| Scale.jobs | $199-$1,099 one-time | Human-assisted | Yes | Expensive |

---

## Open Questions Before Decision
1. Can Playwright + residential proxies bypass Indeed/LinkedIn successfully in 2026? (Need a 2-week test)
2. Do the existing GitHub repos have salvageable code worth building from?
3. What job boards have official APIs that could be used instead of scraping?
4. Is Zach willing to run a product where auto-apply works sometimes but not always — and still charge for it like competitors do?

---

*Research by Atlas | Strategy by Wally | April 21, 2026*
*All competitor data from public sources — verify before making business decisions*

---

## Updated Direction: Path B First (April 21, 2026)

**Decision:** Start with resume optimization, ATS keyword scoring, cover letter generation, and job application tracking. No auto-apply at launch. Same model as Teal but built leaner and bootstrapped.

**Why this works without VC:**
Teal raised .3M to grow to 2M users fast — paying for engineers, marketing, and user acquisition at scale. That is the VC playbook: lose money now, capture market share, monetize later.

You do not need to do that. A bootstrapped version at 1,000 paying subscribers at /month = K/month. That is a real business that funds itself. You do not need 2 million users. You need a few thousand who pay reliably.

**What Teal uses VC for:**
- Engineering salaries (large team)
- Marketing/paid user acquisition
- Integrations (Coursera, Udemy skill recommendations)
- Sales team for enterprise deals
- Runway while not profitable

**What you have instead:**
- Patch builds the product (no K/year engineer salary)
- AI APIs are cheap (/bin/zsh.01-0.03 per resume analysis)
- No office, no headcount
- Existing GitHub code from Octaply to start from
- Your professional network for early users

**Competitive edge over Teal:**
Do not compete on features — compete on execution quality and AI accuracy. Better resume scoring, sharper keyword matching, faster UI. That is where Claude/GPT give a real edge without needing VC.

**Revised MVP Feature List:**
1. Resume builder with AI optimization
2. ATS keyword scoring (job description vs resume gap analysis)
3. Cover letter generator (tailored per job)
4. Job application tracker dashboard (applied, interviewing, offer, rejected)
5. Chrome extension to save jobs from any board
6. LinkedIn profile review

**Pricing:**
- Free: Basic resume builder, 3 job saves, limited keyword analysis
- Pro (-19/month): Unlimited everything, full keyword matching, cover letters, tracker

**First milestone:** 100 paying users. Validate before scaling.
apps.notes: Zach's previous company. GitHub repos with original code still exist. Shut down due to developer cost and technical volatility of bypassing job board security. Core tension: the most valuable feature (auto-apply) is also the hardest to build reliably and carries legal risk.
